covered_sections: 2.1 Vectors and Types of Vectors>Collinear vectors
2.1 Vectors and Types of Vectors>Co-planar vectors
2.1 Vectors and Types of Vectors>Orthogonal vectors
2.1 Vectors and Types of Vectors>Unit vector

mini_headings_covered: describe difference between vector and scalar

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Collinear vectors are vectors that lie along the same line or along parallel lines. Which of the following statements about collinear vectors is correct?

options.A: They must have the same magnitude
options.B: They must be perpendicular to each other
options.C: They lie on lines that are parallel or coincident
options.D: They must point in the same direction

questions.hint: Collinear means on the same line or parallel lines.
questions.explanation: Collinear vectors lie on parallel or coincident lines. Option A is false (magnitudes can differ). B describes perpendicular, not collinear. D describes parallel (same direction) but collinear includes antiparallel.

source_trace.content_anchor: vectors that lie along the same line or parallel lines

explanations_tiered.definition: Collinear vectors are vectors that lie on the same straight line or on parallel lines.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Collinearity does not require same direction or magnitude; vectors may be parallel or antiparallel.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Collinear vectors all lie on the same line (or parallel lines), like train tracks.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Two vectors are collinear if they are scalar multiples: \(\vec{A} = c\vec{B}\) for some real c (positive or negative).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Collinear vectors have zero cross product: \(\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = 0\). They span a one-dimensional subspace.
reasoning_steps: Check if vectors lie on same or parallel lines.
If yes, they are collinear regardless of direction or magnitude.
misconceptions: Some think collinear requires same direction.
Others think collinear vectors must have equal magnitude.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors lying on the same line or parallel lines.

flashcard.back: They lie on the same or parallel lines (any direction, any magnitude).

visual_system.visual_description: Three vectors on parallel horizontal lines: one right, one left, one right with different length.
visual_system.caption: Collinear vectors: same or parallel lines
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Horizontal arrows on three parallel lines.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Vectors that lie on the same or parallel lines.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors on same or parallel lines.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that antiparallel vectors are also collinear.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Collinear = same or parallel lines.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three or more vectors lying in the same plane or parallel to the same plane are called coplanar vectors. Which set of vectors is coplanar?

options.A: Vectors lying in the xy-plane, xz-plane, and yz-plane
options.B: Vectors all lying in the xy-plane only
options.C: Vectors all perpendicular to each other
options.D: Vectors all pointing in the same direction

questions.hint: Coplanar means a single plane contains all vectors.
questions.explanation: Vectors all lying in the xy-plane are coplanar (same plane). Option A spans multiple planes; C and D do not guarantee coplanarity (perpendicular vectors in 3D are not coplanar).

source_trace.content_anchor: three or more vectors lying in the same plane or parallel to the same plane

explanations_tiered.definition: Coplanar vectors lie in the same geometric plane (or parallel planes).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Three or more vectors are coplanar if they can be drawn on a single flat surface without leaving the plane.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Coplanar vectors can all be drawn on the same sheet of paper without any sticking out.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Coplanar vectors have a zero scalar triple product and do not span the full 3D space.
reasoning_steps: Check if all vectors can be placed in a single plane.
misconceptions: Some think perpendicular vectors cannot be coplanar (they can if in same plane).
Some confuse coplanar with collinear.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors that lie in the same plane.

flashcard.front: What does coplanar mean for vectors?
flashcard.back: All vectors lie in the same plane.

visual_system.visual_description: Three vectors drawn on a flat rectangular plane surface.
visual_system.caption: Coplanar vectors: all in same plane
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Three arrows on a flat rectangle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: When they all lie in the same plane.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors lying in the same plane.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. All vectors in xy-plane are coplanar.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Orthogonal vectors are vectors that are perpendicular to each other. Which condition must orthogonal vectors satisfy?
questions.explanation: Orthogonal vectors have dot product zero: \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0\). Option B is zero for parallel/collinear, not perpendicular. C and D are not required for orthogonality.

source_trace.content_anchor: vectors that are perpendicular to one another

explanations_tiered.definition: Orthogonal vectors are perpendicular; their dot product equals zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Orthogonality is a key concept in vector geometry, generalizing perpendicularity to higher dimensions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two arrows at a right angle are orthogonal — their dot product is zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In vector spaces, orthogonal vectors satisfy \(\langle \vec{A},\vec{B}\rangle = 0\). For nonzero vectors, orthogonality implies they are linearly independent.
misconceptions: Some confuse orthogonal with parallel (dot product ≠ 0).
Some think equal magnitude is required.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors that are perpendicular; dot product zero.

flashcard.front: How do you test if two vectors are orthogonal?
flashcard.back: Check if their dot product equals zero.

visual_system.visual_description: Two perpendicular arrows: one horizontal right, one vertical up.
visual_system.caption: Orthogonal vectors: dot product = 0
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Right angle with horizontal and vertical arrows.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors with zero dot product; perpendicular.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Orthogonal means dot product = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A unit vector is a vector that has a magnitude of exactly 1 and has no units. Which statement about unit vectors is true?

options.A: Unit vectors only point along coordinate axes
options.B: Any vector can be written as its magnitude times a unit vector in its direction
options.C: Unit vectors always have the same magnitude as the original vector
options.D: Unit vectors cannot be added to other vectors

questions.explanation: Any vector \(\vec{A}\) can be expressed as \(A\hat{u}\) where \(\hat{u}\) is a unit vector in the same direction. Option A is false (unit vectors can point any direction). C and D are false.

source_trace.content_anchor: vector which has a magnitude of unit length

explanations_tiered.definition: A unit vector has magnitude 1 and indicates direction only, with no units.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Unit vectors are used to separate magnitude from direction: \(\vec{A} = A\hat{u}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A unit vector is like a compass arrow that shows direction but has a fixed length of 1.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given any non-zero vector \(\vec{A}\), the unit vector in its direction is \(\hat{u} = \frac{\vec{A}}{|\vec{A}|}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Unit vectors form an orthonormal basis when orthogonal. Examples: \(\hat{i}, \hat{j}\) in 2D Cartesian coordinates.
misconceptions: Some think unit vectors must point along x or y axes only.
Some believe unit vectors have units.

glossary_entry.definition: A vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction.

flashcard.back: A vector with magnitude exactly 1, showing direction only.

visual_system.visual_description: Original vector A and its unit vector u in same direction, u shorter (length 1).
visual_system.caption: \(\hat{u} = \vec{A}/|\vec{A}|\) has magnitude 1
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two arrows same direction; original longer, unit shorter.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is any vector expressed using a unit vector?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(\vec{A} = A\hat{u}\), where \(\hat{u}\) is unit vector in same direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that \(\hat{i}, \hat{j}\) are specific unit vectors along axes.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Any vector = magnitude × unit vector in that direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the result of adding a vector to the negative of itself?

options.A: A vector with twice the magnitude
options.B: A vector perpendicular to the original
options.D: A vector with the same magnitude but opposite direction

questions.explanation: Adding a vector to its negative gives the zero vector because they cancel exactly. Option A is false (that would be \(\vec{A}+\vec{A}\)). B and D are incorrect results.

source_trace.content_anchor: What is the result of adding a vector to the negative of itself?

explanations_tiered.definition: Adding a vector to its negative yields the zero vector (additive identity).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The negative vector has same magnitude but opposite direction, so they cancel completely.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 5 steps forward and 5 steps backward, you end up where you started — zero displacement.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This demonstrates the existence of additive inverses in vector spaces: \(\vec{v} + (-\vec{v}) = \vec{0}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: negative vector has same magnitude, opposite direction.
Add the two vectors: they cancel exactly.
misconceptions: Some think the result is the original vector.
Some believe magnitude doubles.

glossary_entry.definition: The vector that when added to a given vector yields the zero vector.

visual_system.visual_description: Two arrows of equal length: one right, one left, starting from same point. No net arrow.
visual_system.caption: \(\vec{A} + (-\vec{A}) = \vec{0}\)
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Equal-length arrows pointing opposite directions cancel.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vector that cancels another when added.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. They cancel to give zero vector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When drawing vectors graphically, a scale diagram is used. Why is a scale diagram essential for accurate vector addition?

options.A: It allows vectors of different units to be added
options.B: It ensures that magnitude and direction are represented proportionally
options.C: It eliminates the need for trigonometry

questions.hint: Scale preserves proportions between drawing and reality.
questions.explanation: A scale diagram ensures that vector lengths are proportional to their magnitudes and angles are preserved. Option A is false (units must match). C and D are incorrect; trigonometry may still be needed.

source_trace.content_anchor: Why do we use of scale diagram in vector drawing?

explanations_tiered.definition: A scale diagram maps real vector magnitudes to proportional drawing lengths.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Scaling allows graphical addition by preserving ratios: 1 cm : k units keeps relative sizes correct.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Using a scale (like 1 cm = 10 km) makes sure longer vectors in real life are longer on paper.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Scale ensures that if one vector is twice as long as another in reality, its drawn length is also twice as long.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scale diagrams are similarity transformations: they preserve angles and length ratios, enabling accurate graphical resultant determination.
reasoning_steps: Draw each vector with length = magnitude / scale.
Measure resultant length and convert back using scale.
misconceptions: Some think scale eliminates the need for protractors (false — angles must still be measured).
Some forget to convert back from drawing length to actual magnitude.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scale ensures drawing reflects true magnitudes proportionally.

glossary_entry.definition: A drawing where lengths are proportional to vector magnitudes.

flashcard.front: Why use a scale in vector diagrams?
flashcard.back: To represent magnitudes proportionally and accurately.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the purpose of a scale in vector diagrams?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: To represent magnitudes proportionally to actual values.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Drawing where lengths are proportional to vector magnitudes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Give example: 1 cm = 10 km, 3 cm = 30 km.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain use of scale in vector diagrams
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Scale ensures proportional representation of magnitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: From Example 2.6: Vector \(\vec{A}\) has magnitude 100 m at 50° above +x axis. Vector \(\vec{B}\) has magnitude 200 m at 30° above -x axis (150° from +x). What is the magnitude of the resultant vector \(R\)?
questions.hint: \(R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}\) with \(R_x = A_x + B_x\), \(R_y = A_y + B_y\).

source_trace.content_anchor: A=100 m at 50°, B=200 m at 30° above -x axis, resultant magnitude 207.5 m

explanations_tiered.definition: Resultant magnitude is found by summing components then using Pythagorean theorem.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Component method: resolve each vector, sum x and y separately, then R = √(Rx²+Ry²).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Break each vector into left/right and up/down parts. Add left/right parts, add up/down parts, then use Pythagorean theorem.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The component method is coordinate-independent: \(R = \sqrt{(\sum A\cos\theta)^2 + (\sum A\sin\theta)^2}\).
reasoning_steps: Resolve each vector into x and y components.
misconceptions: Some forget to use correct sign for B_x (cos150° negative).
Some add magnitudes directly (300 m).

glossary_entry.definition: Length of vector sum of multiple vectors.

visual_system.visual_description: Vector A at 50°, vector B at 150°, resultant R from origin to (Rx,Ry).
visual_system.caption: Resultant magnitude = 207.5 m
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two vectors and their resultant in the second quadrant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: R magnitude for A=100 at 50°, B=200 at 150°?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vector sum of two or more vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Highlight that B_x is negative because 150° is in quadrant II.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: From Example 2.7, a girl walks 72 m at 32° East of North, then 57 m at 37° South of West, then 18 m South. Using the component table, what is the x-component (east/west) of the resultant displacement?
questions.hint: East is positive x; West is negative x.
questions.explanation: From table: S1x = 72sin32° = 38.2 m (E), S2x = -57cos37° = -45.6 m (W), S3x = 0. Sum = 38.2 - 45.6 = -7.4 m (west). Option A is only S1x, C is only S2x, D is incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: S1: 72 m at 32° E of N, S2: 57 m at 37° S of W, S3: 18 m S, Rx = -7.4 m

explanations_tiered.definition: The x-component of resultant is the algebraic sum of individual x-components.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: East is positive x, West is negative x. For vectors not aligned with axes, use sine/cosine appropriately.
explanations_tiered.beginner: For 32° East of North, the east part uses sine. For 37° South of West, the west part uses cosine (negative).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The component table method systematically organizes vector resolution for any number of vectors, handling sign conventions consistently.
worked_solution: Step 1: S1: 72 m at 32° E of N → S1x = 72sin32° = 38.2 m.
Step 2: S2: 57 m at 37° S of W → S2x = -57cos37° = -45.6 m.
reasoning_steps: Compute x-component for each vector using sine or cosine based on reference.
misconceptions: Some use cosine for east-of-north incorrectly.
Some forget negative sign for west direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Organized method for calculating resultant components by summing individual components.

flashcard.front: How to find x-component of vector 72 m at 32° E of N?

visual_system.visual_description: Table with rows S1, S2, S3 and columns for x-component (m) and y-component (m).
visual_system.caption: Components of displacement vectors
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Table showing S1x=38.2, S2x=-45.6, S3x=0 → Rx=-7.4 m.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Rx for 72 m at 32° E of N, 57 m at 37° S of W, 18 m S?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Organized method for vector component sums.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Rx = Σ(±A sin or cos θ).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize angle conventions (E of N uses sine for x).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use component table for vector addition

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Under what condition does the vector sum of two non-zero vectors become a null (zero) vector?

options.B: When they have equal magnitude and opposite direction
options.D: When one is twice the other

questions.explanation: Two non-zero vectors sum to zero only if they have equal magnitude and opposite direction (\(\vec{A} + (-\vec{A}) = \vec{0}\)). Option A gives non-zero sum (√2 times magnitude). C and D give non-zero results.

source_trace.content_anchor: State the condition where the vector sum of two vectors is a null vector

explanations_tiered.definition: Two non-zero vectors sum to zero if and only if they are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This is the condition for a balanced pair: \(\vec{A} + \vec{B} = 0 \iff \vec{B} = -\vec{A}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 5 steps north and 5 steps south, you end up back where you started.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For non-zero vectors, \(\vec{A} + \vec{B} = \vec{0}\) means \(\vec{B} = -\vec{A}\). Thus they are antiparallel with equal magnitudes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is the definition of additive inverses in vector spaces. No other combination of non-zero vectors yields the null vector.
reasoning_steps: Thus \(\vec{B}\) has same magnitude as \(\vec{A}\) and opposite direction.
misconceptions: Some think perpendicular vectors can sum to zero (they cannot for non-zero vectors).
Some believe any two opposite vectors cancel regardless of magnitude.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector sum equal to zero vector; occurs for equal-magnitude opposite-direction vectors.

flashcard.front: When do two non-zero vectors sum to zero?
flashcard.back: When equal magnitude and opposite direction.

visual_system.visual_description: Two arrows of equal length pointing opposite directions from same point. No resultant.
visual_system.caption: \(\vec{A} + (-\vec{A}) = \vec{0}\)
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two equal-length arrows: one right, one left, canceling.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Condition for two non-zero vectors to sum to zero?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Resultant of zero vector from balanced forces.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Analogous to tug-of-war with equal force from both sides.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Equal magnitude and opposite direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A machine applies a force \(\vec{F} = (50\hat{i} + 30\hat{j})\mathrm{N}\) to move a body with average velocity \(\vec{V} = (3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j})\mathrm{m/s}\). What is the power developed by the machine?
questions.explanation: \(P = (50)(3) + (30)(4) = 150 + 120 = 270\mathrm{W}\). Option A is only F_x·V_x; C is only F_y·V_y; D is sum of magnitudes.

explanations_tiered.definition: Power is the dot product of force and velocity vectors: \(P = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{V}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Power measures the rate at which work is done. In vector form, only the force component along velocity contributes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply matching components of force and velocity, then add the results.
explanations_tiered.advanced: \(P = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{V} = |\vec{F}||\vec{V}|\cos\theta\), where θ is angle between force and velocity. Here, \(\cos\theta = \frac{50*3+30*4}{\sqrt{50^2+30^2}\sqrt{3^2+4^2}} = \frac{270}{58.3\times5} \approx 0.926\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify components: Fx=50 N, Fy=30 N; Vx=3 m/s, Vy=4 m/s.
reasoning_steps: Write force and velocity vectors in component form.
Compute dot product: sum of products of corresponding components.
misconceptions: Some multiply magnitudes: √(50²+30²)×√(3²+4²)=58.3×5=291.5 W (wrong).
Some add all components: 50+30+3+4=87 (nonsense).

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of work; dot product of force and velocity.

flashcard.front: Power formula using force and velocity vectors?

concept_map_nodes.target: Dot product of Force and Velocity

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Power from F=(50i+30j) N, v=(3i+4j) m/s?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rate of doing work; dot product of force and velocity.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students that only force component parallel to velocity does work.
